https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_(progra:
Programming language
For the 2003 agent-based programming language, see[Go! (programming language)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go!_\(programming_language\) "Go! \(programming language\)").
Implementation language Go was designed at[Google](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Google "Google")in 2007 to improve[programming productivity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Programming_productivity "Programming productivity")in an era of[multicore](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multi-core_processor "Multi-core processor"),[networked](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_network "Computer network")[machines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer "Computer")and large[codebases](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Codebase "Codebase").[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-23)The designers wanted to address criticisms of other languages in use at Google, but keep their useful characteristics:[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-24) In retrospect the Go authors judged Go to be successful due to the overall engineering work around[_[clarification needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")_]the language, including the runtime support for the language's concurrency feature. Although the design of most languages concentrates on innovations in syntax, semantics, or typing, Go is focused on the software development process itself. ... The principal unusual property of the language itself—concurrency—addressed problems that arose with the proliferation of multicore CPUs in the 2010s. But more significant was the early work that established fundamentals for packaging, dependencies, build, test, deployment, and other workaday tasks of the software development world, aspects that are not usually foremost in language design.[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-33) Go 1 guarantees compatibility[[47]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-47)for the language specification and major parts of the standard library. All versions up through the current Go 1.26 release[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-48)have maintained this promise.
* A desire to keep the language specification simple enough to hold in a programmer's head,[[56]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-56)in part by[omitting features that are common in similar languages](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#Omissions).
For a pair of types`K`,`V`, the type`map[K]V`is the type mapping type-`K`keys to type-`V`values, which can be thought of as equivalent to`Map`in other languages. The Go Programming Language specification does not give any performance guarantees or implementation requirements for map types, though it is usually implemented as a[hash table](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hash_table "Hash table")(equivalent to`HashMap`in other languages). Hash tables are built into the language, with special syntax and built-in functions.`chan T`is a _channel_ that allows sending values of type`T`between[concurrent Go processes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#Concurrency).[[69]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-73) Besides calling methods via interfaces, Go allows converting interface values to other types with a run-time type check. The language constructs to do so are the _type assertion_ ,[[81]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-85)which checks against a single potential type: The Go language has built-in facilities, as well as library support, for writing[concurrent programs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Concurrent_programming "Concurrent programming"). The runtime is[asynchronous](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asynchronous_I/O "Asynchronous I/O"): program execution that performs, for example, a network read will be suspended until data is available to process, allowing other parts of the program to perform other work. This is built into the runtime and does not require any changes in program code. The go runtime also automatically schedules concurrent operations (goroutines) across multiple CPUs; this can achieve parallelism for a properly written program.[[97]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-concurrency-is-not-101) The existence of channels does not by itself set Go apart from[actor model](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Actor_model "Actor model")-style concurrent languages like Erlang, where messages are addressed directly to actors (corresponding to goroutines). In the actor model, channels are themselves actors, therefore addressing a channel just means to address an actor. The actor style can be simulated in Go by maintaining a one-to-one correspondence between goroutines and channels, but the language allows multiple goroutines to share a channel or a single goroutine to send and receive on multiple channels.[[99]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-phrasebook-103): 147 Concurrency-related structural conventions of Go ([channels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Channel_\(programming\) "Channel \(programming\)")and alternative channel inputs) are derived from[Tony Hoare's](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C._A._R._Hoare "C. A. R. Hoare")[communicating sequential processes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Communicating_sequential_processes "Communicating sequential processes")model. Unlike previous concurrent programming languages such as[Occam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Occam_\(programming_language\) "Occam \(programming language\)")or[Limbo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Limbo_\(programming_language\) "Limbo \(programming language\)")(a language on which Go co-designer Rob Pike worked),[[107]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-111)Go does not provide any built-in notion of safe or verifiable concurrency.[[108]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-memmodel-112)While the communicating-processes model is favored in Go, it is not the only one: all goroutines in a program share a single address space. This means that mutable objects and pointers can be shared between goroutines; see[§ Lack of data race safety](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#Lack_of_data_race_safety), below.
#### Suitability for parallel programming
Although Go's concurrency features are not aimed primarily at[parallel processing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parallel_computing "Parallel computing"),[[97]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-concurrency-is-not-101)they can be used to program[shared-memory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shared-memory_architecture "Shared-memory architecture")[multi-processor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multiprocessing "Multiprocessing")machines. Various studies have been done into the effectiveness of this approach.[[109]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-113)One of these studies compared the size (in[lines of code](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lines_of_code "Lines of code")) and speed of programs written by a seasoned programmer not familiar with the language and corrections to these programs by a Go expert (from Google's development team), doing the same for[Chapel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chapel_\(programming_language\) "Chapel \(programming language\)"),[Cilk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cilk "Cilk")and[Intel TBB](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intel_Threading_Building_Blocks "Intel Threading Building Blocks"). The study found that the non-expert tended to write[divide-and-conquer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fork%E2%80%93join_model "Fork–join model")algorithms with one`go`statement per recursion, while the expert wrote distribute-work-synchronize programs using one goroutine per processor core. The expert's programs were usually faster, but also longer.[[110]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-114)
* The _omission_ of certain features (for example, functional-programming shortcuts like`map`and Java-style`try`/`finally`blocks) tends to encourage a particular explicit, concrete, and imperative programming style.
Another tool maintained by the Go team but is not included in Go distributions is`gopls`, a language server that provides[IDE](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Integrated_development_environment "Integrated development environment")features such as[intelligent code completion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intelligent_code_completion "Intelligent code completion")to[Language Server Protocol](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Language_Server_Protocol "Language Server Protocol")compatible editors.[[134]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-142) The interface system, and the deliberate omission of inheritance, were praised by Michele Simionato, who likened these characteristics to those of[Standard ML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_ML "Standard ML"), calling it "a shame that no popular language has followed [this] particular route".[[144]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-152) Go is extremely easy to dive into. There are a minimal number of fundamental language concepts and the[syntax](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Syntax_\(programming_languages\) "Syntax \(programming languages\)")is clean and designed to be clear and unambiguous. Go _is_ still experimental and still a little rough around the edges. Go was named Programming Language of the Year by the[TIOBE Programming Community Index](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TIOBE_Programming_Community_Index "TIOBE Programming Community Index")in its first year, 2009, for having a larger 12-month increase in popularity (in only 2 months, after its introduction in November) than any other language that year, and reached 13th place by January 2010,[[146]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-154)surpassing established languages like[Pascal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pascal_\(programming_language\) "Pascal \(programming language\)"). By June 2015, its ranking had dropped to below 50th in the index, placing it lower than[COBOL](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COBOL "COBOL")and[Fortran](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fortran "Fortran").[[147]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-155)But as of January 2017, its ranking had surged to 13th, indicating significant growth in popularity and adoption. Go was again awarded TIOBE Programming Language of the Year in 2016.[[148]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-156) A 2011 evaluation of the language and itsgcimplementation in comparison to C++ ([GCC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GNU_Compiler_Collection "GNU Compiler Collection")), Java and[Scala](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scala_\(programming_language\) "Scala \(programming language\)")by a Google engineer found: Go offers interesting language features, which also allow for a concise and standardized notation. The compilers for this language are still immature, which reflects in both performance and binary sizes.
* Go has been criticized for focusing on simplicity of implementation rather than correctness and flexibility; as an example, the language uses[POSIX](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/POSIX "POSIX")file semantics on all platforms, and therefore provides incorrect information on platforms such as[Windows](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows")(which do not follow the aforementioned standard).[[154]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-162)[[155]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-163)
On November 10, 2009, the day of the general release of the language, Francis McCabe, developer of the[Go! programming language](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go!_\(programming_language\) "Go! \(programming language\)")(note the exclamation point), requested a name change of Google's language to prevent confusion with his language, which he had spent 10 years developing.[[157]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-165)McCabe raised concerns that "the 'big guy' will end up steam-rollering over" him, and this concern resonated with the more than 120 developers who commented on Google's official issues thread saying they should change the name, with some[[158]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-infoweek-166)even saying the issue contradicts Google's motto of:[Don't be evil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Don%27t_be_evil "Don't be evil").[[159]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_\(programming_language\)#cite_note-issue9-167)
